title: Correlation wheel plots with circlecorR
text: traditional correlation matrix carries large amount redundant information. kk variables k2k^2 cells, k(k−1)/2k(k-1)/2 unique: diagonal self-correlations (r=1r = 1), two triangles mirror , large blocks within-category correlations (symptom–symptom, metric–metric) often question asked. kk grows, interesting -category relationships buried redundancy figure becomes unreadable. correlation wheel introduced Gharibans colleagues cut : variables sit around circle, grouped category, correlations interest drawn curved links coloured coefficient (Gharibans et al. 2019). circlecorR reproduces figure natively R makes grouping, colours, statistics configurable.

what: The simplest path: straight from your data
title: Correlation wheel plots with circlecorR
text: datasets one row per subject one column per variable. need build correlation matrices – hand data frame directly corr_wheel() computes correlations (p-values) . thing supply groups: named list mapping category variables. selects variables appear (columns, ID, ignored) sets order around wheel.
code: groups <- list(   Demographics = c("Age", "BMI"),   Metrics      = c("Amplitude", "Fed-Fasted AR", "Frequency", "GA-RI"),   Symptoms     = c("Nausea", "Early satiety", "Bloating",                    "Upper GI pain", "Lower GI pain", "Heartburn"),   Scores       = c("GCSI", "PAGI-SYM", "PAGI-QoL", "EQ-5D") ) # `gastro_symptoms` is a synthetic per-row example dataset shipped with the package head(gastro_symptoms[, 1:5]) #>   Age  BMI Amplitude Fed-Fasted AR Frequency #> 1  74 41.0  30.26932     2.0004345  2.808546 #> 2  51 42.8  29.15670     0.7884043  1.760702 #> 3  59 36.2  29.73068     0.1899037  3.483917 #> 4  47 40.8  31.16330    -0.8294263  2.842009 #> 5  29 46.1  31.07111     0.9579552  4.015299 #> 6  50 36.4  29.86780    -2.0601868  1.179358  corr_wheel(   gastro_symptoms,             # raw data: one row per subject   groups      = groups,   method      = "pearson",     # correlation method   adjust      = "hochberg",    # multiple-comparison adjustment (see below)   sig_level   = 0.05,          # hide links with adjusted p > 0.05   r_threshold = 0.3,           # ...and links with |r| < 0.3   r_limits    = c(-0.6, 0.6) )

title: Correlation wheel plots with circlecorR
text: Two wheel’s important features never draws self-correlations (diagonal) , default, hides within-category correlations (hide_within_group = TRUE). removes exactly redundant parts matrix leaves -category structure. Crucially, carried statistics. Multiple-comparison correction penalises number hypotheses tested. self- within-category correlations never tested, count towards family. corr_wheel() therefore applies adjustment correlations displays. Shrinking family makes correction less severe – power improves – remaining statistically consistent shown.  family smaller, raw p-value corrected smaller factor. Taking Bonferroni transparent example, correlation penalised number tests family – 92 rather 120: step-methods ("holm", "hochberg", "BH") behave way: fewer comparisons never gives larger adjusted p-value, hiding redundant self- within-category correlations can help power. pass pre-computed p-value matrix instead raw data, taken given (re-adjustment), since may already corrected.
code:
res <- corr_wheel(gastro_symptoms, groups = groups, adjust = "hochberg",                   r_threshold = 0.3, r_limits = c(-0.6, 0.6)) k <- length(unlist(groups)) cat("Unique correlations in the full matrix:", k * (k - 1) / 2, "
") #> Unique correlations in the full matrix: 120 cat("Correlations actually tested (the family):", res$n_tests, "
") #> Correlations actually tested (the family): 92 p_raw <- 5e-4                       # a raw p-value for one correlation k_all <- k * (k - 1) / 2  cat("Bonferroni across the full matrix:", signif(p_raw * k_all, 3), "
") #> Bonferroni across the full matrix: 0.06 cat("Bonferroni across the family only:", signif(p_raw * res$n_tests, 3), "
") #> Bonferroni across the family only: 0.046

title: Correlation wheel plots with circlecorR
text: corr_wheel() auto-detects pass: raw data (non-square) – correlations computed ; correlation matrix plus matching p matrix; circlecor object compute_correlations().
code: r <- as.matrix(read.csv("Rvalues.csv", row.names = 1)) p <- as.matrix(read.csv("Pvalues.csv", row.names = 1)) corr_wheel(r, p, groups = groups, r_threshold = 0.3)

title: Correlation wheel plots with circlecorR
text: colors maps categories colours; labels gives pretty display names. named vectors – specify ones want change.
code: corr_wheel(   gastro_symptoms, groups = groups, r_threshold = 0.3, r_limits = c(-0.6, 0.6),   colors = c(Demographics = "#4C72B0", Metrics = "#DD8452",              Symptoms = "#55A868", Scores = "#C44E52"),   labels = c(`GA-RI` = "Rhythm index") )

title: Correlation wheel plots with circlecorR
text: tile_height – radial thickness category blocks (smaller = thinner). link_lwd – line width links (larger = thicker).
code: corr_wheel(   gastro_symptoms, groups = groups, r_threshold = 0.3, r_limits = c(-0.6, 0.6),   tile_height = 0.12,   # thicker blocks   link_lwd    = 3       # thicker lines )

title: Correlation wheel plots with circlecorR
text: palette sets three colours c(-limit, 0, +limit) r_limits scale range.
code: corr_wheel(   gastro_symptoms, groups = groups, r_threshold = 0.3,   palette  = c("#2166AC", "white", "#B2182B"),   # blue - white - red   r_limits = c(-0.5, 0.5) )

title: Correlation wheel plots with circlecorR
text: corr_wheel() draws active graphics device, save usual way:
code: png("correlation_wheel.png", width = 2500, height = 2000, res = 300) corr_wheel(gastro_symptoms, groups = groups, r_threshold = 0.3,            r_limits = c(-0.6, 0.6)) dev.off()

code: @Manual{,   title = {circlecorR: Circular Correlation Wheel Plots},   author = {Chris Varghese and Armen A Gharibans},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://kriz98.github.io/circlecorR/}, }

title: Circular Correlation Wheel Plots
text: Circular correlation “wheel” plots native R. circlecorR draws correlation wheel plots — variables arranged around ring, grouped colour-tiled category, connected curved links whose colour maps correlation coefficient — using circlize. replaces older R → Python (mne.viz.plot_connectivity_circle) round-trip single R call. traditional correlation matrix mostly redundant: diagonal self-correlations, two mirror-image triangles, blocks within-category correlations bury -category relationships care . wheel — introduced Gharibans et al. (2019) — keeps relationships. Hiding self- within-category correlations also removes multiple-comparison family, improves statistical power (see vignette("circlecorR")).

title: Circular Correlation Wheel Plots
text: (CRAN release pending.) needs circlize; psych required let package compute correlations (raw-data path ).

what: Quick start — straight from your data
title: Circular Correlation Wheel Plots
text: datasets one row per subject, one column per variable. don’t need build correlation matrices : hand data frame corr_wheel() computes correlations p-values . thing supply groups, selects variables orders around wheel (extra columns like IDs ignored).
code: library(circlecorR)  groups <- list(   Demographics = c("Age", "BMI"),   Metrics      = c("Amplitude", "Fed-Fasted AR", "Frequency", "GA-RI"),   Symptoms     = c("Nausea", "Early satiety", "Bloating",                    "Upper GI pain", "Lower GI pain", "Heartburn"),   Scores       = c("GCSI", "PAGI-SYM", "PAGI-QoL", "EQ-5D") )  corr_wheel(   my_data,                 # one row per subject   groups      = groups,   method      = "pearson",   adjust      = "hochberg",   sig_level   = 0.05,   r_threshold = 0.3,   r_limits    = c(-0.6, 0.6) )

code: r <- as.matrix(read.csv("Rvalues.csv", row.names = 1)) p <- as.matrix(read.csv("Pvalues.csv", row.names = 1)) corr_wheel(r, p, groups = groups, r_threshold = 0.3)

title: Circular Correlation Wheel Plots
text: Everything reference figure controls plain argument: corr_wheel() returns (invisibly) ordered variables, resolved group colour maps, colour function, masked matrix actually plotted — handy reproducibility building caption.

title: Circular Correlation Wheel Plots
text: gastro_symptoms (raw) gastro_cor (r/p circlecor object) bundled synthetic datasets — fully simulated, patient data — mimicking gastric -symptom study. See ?gastro_cor.

title: Circular Correlation Wheel Plots
text: link variables j drawn hold: different categories (hide_within_group = TRUE), never variable (self-correlations always excluded); p_adjusted <= sig_level; |r| >= r_threshold. correlations left step 1 form family comparisons. p-values raw (computed data, circlecor object), adjust correction applied family — redundant self- within-category correlations don’t inflate count, power improves. pre-computed p matrix used given. corr_wheel() returns n_tests (family size) family-adjusted p-matrix reporting.

title: Circular Correlation Wheel Plots
text: Gharibans AA, Coleman TP, Mousa H, Kunkel DC. Spatial patterns high-resolution electrogastrography correlate severity symptoms patients functional dyspepsia gastroparesis. Clin Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2019 Dec;17(13):2668–77.

what: circlecorR: Circular Correlation Wheel Plots — circlecorR-package
title: circlecorR: Circular Correlation Wheel Plots — circlecorR-package
text: Draws MNE-style circular "correlation wheel" plots natively R. main entry point corr_wheel(); compute_correlations() builds r/p input raw data.

title: circlecorR: Circular Correlation Wheel Plots — circlecorR-package
text: Maintainer: Chris Varghese chris.varghese@alimetry.com Authors: Chris Varghese chris.varghese@alimetry.com Armen Gharibans

what: Compute a correlation matrix and (raw) p-values from raw data — compute_correlations
title: Compute a correlation matrix and (raw) p-values from raw data — compute_correlations
text: thin wrapper around psych::corr.test() returns correlation (r) unadjusted p-value (p) matrices bundled single object suitable passing straight corr_wheel().

title: Compute a correlation matrix and (raw) p-values from raw data — compute_correlations
code: compute_correlations(   data,   vars = NULL,   method = c("pearson", "spearman", "kendall"),   use = "pairwise.complete.obs" )

title: Compute a correlation matrix and (raw) p-values from raw data — compute_correlations
text: data data frame matrix observations (rows) variables (columns). vars Optional character vector selecting ordering columns data use. Defaults columns. method Correlation method, passed psych::corr.test(). One "pearson", "spearman", "kendall". use Handling missing values, passed psych::corr.test().

title: Compute a correlation matrix and (raw) p-values from raw data — compute_correlations
text: object class "circlecor": list elements r (correlation matrix), p (symmetric matrix raw, unadjusted p-values), n (pairwise sample sizes psych), method.

title: Compute a correlation matrix and (raw) p-values from raw data — compute_correlations
text: Multiple-comparison adjustment deliberately applied . correlation wheel, self-correlations (usually) within-category correlations never tested, count towards family comparisons. corr_wheel() therefore applies adjustment , exactly set correlations displays – statistically consistent powerful adjusting across full matrix. See adjust hide_within_group arguments corr_wheel().

title: Compute a correlation matrix and (raw) p-values from raw data — compute_correlations
code: if (requireNamespace("psych", quietly = TRUE)) {   cc <- compute_correlations(mtcars, method = "pearson")   str(cc) } #> List of 4 #>  $ r     : num [1:11, 1:11] 1 -0.852 -0.848 -0.776 0.681 ... #>   ..- attr(*, "dimnames")=List of 2 #>   .. ..$ : chr [1:11] "mpg" "cyl" "disp" "hp" ... #>   .. ..$ : chr [1:11] "mpg" "cyl" "disp" "hp" ... #>  $ p     : num [1:11, 1:11] 0.00 6.11e-10 9.38e-10 1.79e-07 1.78e-05 ... #>   ..- attr(*, "dimnames")=List of 2 #>   .. ..$ : chr [1:11] "mpg" "cyl" "disp" "hp" ... #>   .. ..$ : chr [1:11] "mpg" "cyl" "disp" "hp" ... #>  $ n     : num 32 #>  $ method: chr "pearson" #>  - attr(*, "class")= chr "circlecor"

what: Draw a circular correlation wheel plot — corr_wheel
title: Draw a circular correlation wheel plot — corr_wheel
text: Arranges variables around circle, grouped colour-tiled category, connects curved links whose colour maps correlation coefficient. Non-significant, weak, (optionally) within-category correlations masked. reproduces MNE-style "connectivity circle" natively R using circlize.

title: Draw a circular correlation wheel plot — corr_wheel
code: corr_wheel(   r,   p = NULL,   groups = NULL,   colors = NULL,   labels = NULL,   order = NULL,   sig_level = 0.05,   r_threshold = 0,   hide_within_group = TRUE,   method = c("pearson", "spearman", "kendall"),   adjust = "holm",   use = "pairwise.complete.obs",   p_from = c("lower", "upper"),   r_limits = c(-0.5, 0.5),   palette = c("#2166AC", "#FFFFFF", "#B2182B"),   start_degree = 90,   group_gap = 5,   node_gap = 1.5,   link_lwd = 1.6,   sort_links = TRUE,   tile_height = 0.06,   label_cex = 0.85,   label_pad = 0.45,   label_r_offset = 0.07,   tile_border = "white",   title = NULL,   legend = TRUE,   colorbar = TRUE,   legend_title = "Category",   colorbar_title = "Correlation\ncoefficient" )

title: Draw a circular correlation wheel plot — corr_wheel
text: r One three things, auto-detected: raw data – data frame matrix one row per observation (e.g. one row per patient) variables columns. Correlations p-values computed via compute_correlations() using method, adjust, use. simplest entry point. correlation matrix (square, variable names dimnames), data frame one; pass matching p-values via p. "circlecor" object compute_correlations(); p matrix used p argument ignored. groups supplied, variables names used (order), extra columns IDs simply ignored. p Optional matching matrix p-values. supplied, links p > sig_level hidden. Ignored raw data "circlecor" inputs (computed instead). groups Category assignment variables. Either named vector (variable = category) named list (category = c(variables)). order categories sets order around wheel. NULL, variables share one group. colors Named vector mapping category colour. Missing categories filled default palette. labels Named vector mapping variable name display label. Missing entries fall back variable name. order Optional character vector giving explicit variable order around wheel. Overrides ordering groups. Must contain every variable. sig_level Significance threshold; links p > sig_level hidden. used p-value matrix available. r_threshold Minimum absolute correlation display. hide_within_group Logical; TRUE (default) correlations two variables category hidden – excluded multiple-comparison family (see Details). Self-correlations (diagonal) always excluded. method, use Passed compute_correlations() r raw data: correlation method missing-value handling. Ignored otherwise. adjust Multiple-comparison adjustment method applied raw p-values displayed family correlations (see Details). method accepted stats::p.adjust() (e.g. "holm", "hochberg", "BH", "bonferroni", "none"). Used p-values raw (raw-data "circlecor" input); user-supplied p matrix taken given. p_from p-value matrix asymmetric (psych), triangle mirror: "lower" (raw p-values, default) "upper" (adjusted). r_limits Length-2 numeric giving colour-scale limits (c(vmin, vmax)). Correlations beyond clamped colour. palette Colours diverging link scale c(r_limits[1], midpoint, r_limits[2]). Default blue-white-red scale. start_degree Angle (degrees) first variable; 90 places top, going clockwise. group_gap, node_gap Gaps (degrees) categories variables within category. link_lwd Line width (size) links. Increase thicker lines. sort_links TRUE (default), stronger correlations drawn last (top). tile_height Radial thickness (size) coloured category blocks. Smaller values give thinner blocks rim. label_cex, tile_border Label text size tile border colour. label_pad Extra canvas padding (fraction circle radius) keep long outer labels clipped. Increase longer labels. label_r_offset Radial gap (circle-radius units) outer edge tiles start labels. title Optional plot title. legend, colorbar Logical toggles category legend correlation colour bar. legend_title, colorbar_title Titles legend colour bar.

title: Draw a circular correlation wheel plot — corr_wheel
text: Invisibly, list ordered vars, resolved groups, colors, col_fun colour mapping, masked matrix correlations actually drawn (others NA), family-adjusted p-value matrix p_adjusted, number drawn links n_links, size comparison family n_tests, adjust method applied.

title: Draw a circular correlation wheel plot — corr_wheel
text: traditional correlation matrix dominated redundant information: diagonal self-correlations, mirror-image lower triangle, blocks within-category correlations rarely interest. wheel keeps correlations actually want inspect – default -category correlations, self- within-category correlations hidden. display choice; carried statistics. p-values computed raw data (supplied via compute_correlations() object), multiple-comparison adjustment (adjust) applied family correlations shown – .e. excluding self- , hide_within_group = TRUE, within-category correlations. redundant comparisons longer count towards family, correction less severe statistical power improves.

title: Draw a circular correlation wheel plot — corr_wheel
code: grp <- list(   Demographics = c("Age", "BMI"),   Metrics      = c("Amplitude", "Fed-Fasted AR", "Frequency", "GA-RI"),   Symptoms     = c("Nausea", "Early satiety", "Bloating", "Upper GI pain",                    "Lower GI pain", "Heartburn"),   Scores       = c("GCSI", "PAGI-SYM", "PAGI-QoL", "EQ-5D") )  # Simplest: straight from a per-row data frame (correlations computed for you) data(gastro_symptoms) if (requireNamespace("psych", quietly = TRUE)) {   corr_wheel(gastro_symptoms, groups = grp, r_threshold = 0.3,              r_limits = c(-0.6, 0.6)) }   # Or from pre-computed matrices / a circlecor object data(gastro_cor) corr_wheel(gastro_cor, groups = grp, r_threshold = 0.3,            r_limits = c(-0.6, 0.6))

what: Correlation matrices for the synthetic gastric-symptom dataset — gastro_cor
title: Correlation matrices for the synthetic gastric-symptom dataset — gastro_cor
text: "circlecor" object (Pearson, Hochberg-adjusted) computed gastro_symptoms compute_correlations(). Ready pass corr_wheel().

title: Correlation matrices for the synthetic gastric-symptom dataset — gastro_cor
Correlation matrices for the synthetic gastric-symptom dataset — gastro_cor
text: list class "circlecor" elements r (16x16 correlation matrix) p (16x16 p-value matrix).

title: Synthetic gastric-symptom dataset — gastro_symptoms
text: fully simulated dataset (patient data) mimicking structure gastric-symptom study: demographics, gastric metrics, symptom sub-scales, summary scores 100 imaginary subjects. Loadings deliberately moderate uneven, realistic subset correlations significant. Generated data-raw/make_example_data.R.

title: Synthetic gastric-symptom dataset — gastro_symptoms
text: data frame 100 rows 16 variables: Age, BMI Demographics. Amplitude, Fed-Fasted AR, Frequency, GA-RI Simulated gastric metrics. Nausea, Early satiety, Bloating, Upper GI pain, Lower GI pain, Heartburn Symptom sub-scale scores. GCSI, PAGI-SYM, PAGI-QoL, EQ-5D Summary symptom quality--life scores.
